title: Transportation Assistance for Olympic and World Cup Cities Act of 2025
short_title: Transportation Assistance for Olympic and World Cup Cities Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill helps American cities that host big international sports events like the Olympics and World Cup. The government will give these cities money and help them improve their transportation systems like buses, trains, and roads. The bill also requires the Department of Commerce to study how hosting these major sports events helps bring more tourists and travelers to the United States. This support makes it easier for cities to handle the large crowds that come to watch these important sporting competitions.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Transportation Assistance for Olympic and World Cup Cities Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Transportation Assistance for International Sporting Events
sections.text: This section changes existing transportation law to help cities that host big international sports events. The purpose is to help state, tribal, and local governments deal with transportation challenges when they host major sporting events. This support helps them get the recognition and economic benefits that come from hosting these events. A covered event means Olympics, Paralympics, Special Olympics, FIFA Men's World Cup, or FIFA Women's World Cup held in the United States or its territories. These events must be officially selected by the International Olympic Committee or FIFA. Multiple events in the same city or area that are part of the same competition count as one covered event. This does not include activities for preparing bids to host these events. An eligible entity means any state, Indian tribe, or local government that hosts or supports a covered event. It also includes port authorities and transit agencies. These entities must own transportation facilities or provide transportation services within one hundred miles of where the event is held. Metropolitan planning organizations that serve these areas can also be eligible entities. Nonprofit groups created to run covered events are also eligible. A host metropolitan planning organization means the planning group for the urban area where the covered event happens. The Secretary of Transportation will give out money each year to help with transportation projects. The money gets divided up fairly among host metropolitan planning organizations for the most urgent covered events happening that year. Each host planning organization can get up to ten million dollars for each event. Once those organizations get their full ten million dollars, any leftover money goes to other host planning organizations for future events. The money can be used for transportation projects within one hundred miles of the event location. These projects must help move people or goods related to the event. The host planning organization decides which projects qualify, but the projects cannot be temporary structures just for the event. Projects must follow the same rules as other federal transportation projects. Organizations can only get this money starting five years before their event begins and ending thirty days after the event ends. The Secretary can give back money for eligible activities during this time period. Any money not spent within one year after the event ends goes back into the pool to be given out again. Fifty million dollars per year is authorized to pay for these grants. The Secretary of Transportation will also provide technical help and planning assistance to eligible entities when they ask for it. This includes help with transportation planning activities, developing transportation plans that connect different types of travel, and helping governments share buses and equipment during events. The Secretary will also speed up reviews of required paperwork related to covered events and help coordinate planning between governments and private companies. The Secretary can provide any other technical help needed to support the purpose of this law. This assistance is only available during the same time period as the grants, from five years before the event starts until thirty days after it ends.

sections.title: Section 3 Studies on the Effects of Hosting the Olympics and World Cup on the Travel and Tourism Industry in the United States
sections.text: This section defines key terms and requires studies about how hosting major sports events affects travel and tourism. Local Planning Committees means the World Cup planning committees for each city that hosts World Cup games. Olympics means the twenty twenty eight Summer Olympics and Paralympics that Los Angeles, California will host. The Secretary means the Secretary of Commerce. Travel and tourism industry means the travel and tourism business in the United States. World Cup means the twenty twenty six Men's FIFA World Cup that the United States, Mexico, and Canada will host together. The Secretary of Commerce must start a study about how the Olympics affects travel and tourism within ninety days after this law is passed. Before starting the study, the Secretary must talk with the United States Travel and Tourism Advisory Board and the United States Olympic Planning Committee. The study will look at how the Olympics changes international travel to the United States, domestic travel within the United States, how much money travel and tourism businesses make, and employment rates in the travel and tourism industry. Within one hundred eighty days after the Olympics ends, the Secretary must give a report with the study results to the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation and the House Committee on Energy and Commerce. The Secretary must also put this report on the Department of Commerce website so the public can read it. The Secretary of Commerce must also start a study about how the World Cup affects travel and tourism within ninety days after this law is passed. Before starting this study, the Secretary must talk with the United States Travel and Tourism Advisory Board and Local Planning Committees. This study will examine the same things as the Olympics study: changes in international travel to the United States, changes in domestic travel within the United States, changes in business revenues in the travel and tourism industry, and changes in employment rates in the travel and tourism industry. Within one hundred eighty days after the World Cup ends, the Secretary must give a report with the study results to the same congressional committees. The Secretary must also put this report on the Department of Commerce website for the public to read.
